concerns.text: FR-008 requires a sensitivity analysis sweeping thresholds over {0.01, 0.05, 0.1} to report 'false-positive/false-negative rates'. However, the spec defines no ground-truth labels for the majority of the dataset (Reddit threads) and explicitly excludes threads without ground truth from predictive accuracy analysis (Edge Cases). Without a known ground truth for the full dataset, calculating 'false-positive/false-negative rates' is mathematically impossible to verify. The requirement is untestable as written.

FR-005 mandates computing 'predictive accuracy against external ground truth'. The Edge Cases state: 'What happens when ground truth is unavailable...? → Predictive accuracy metric is set to null'. If the metric is null for a significant portion of the data (likely Reddit), the requirement to 'compute' it is not verifiable for those instances. The spec must define a verifiable fallback or a minimum threshold of ground-truth availability to consider the FR met.

SC-005 states: 'the full analysis MUST complete within these bounds [GitHub Actions free-tier]'. This is a performance constraint, but the spec lacks a 'load profile' or 'dataset size' definition (e.g., 'for a dataset of N=1000 threads'). Without a defined input size, 'completing within bounds' is not measurable. A script running on 10 threads will pass; one on 1M will fail. The SC needs a specific dataset scale to be testable.

Assumption 2 claims 'Ground truth... is available for ≥50% of selected threads'. This is an unverified assumption about the data source, not a system capability. If the actual data yields <50%, the project fails its own premise. This should be a Success Criterion (e.g., 'SC-006: ≥50% of threads must possess verifiable ground truth') to be measurable before proceeding to analysis.
